title: Multilingual Tokenization
concept: Tokenizing multiple languages fairly requires balanced training data; if the tokenizer is biased toward English, foreign text will break into too many small fragments.

exercises.prompt: What happens to non-English text if a tokenizer is only trained on English?

options.text: It translates everything to English automatically.
Foreign words are chopped into too many tiny pieces.
The AI refuses to read any text at all.

exercises.explain: If a tokenizer isn't trained on diverse languages, it won't recognize their structures, causing it to slice foreign words into inefficiently small pieces.

cards.title: A Fair Share of Words
cards.body: Have you ever wondered how an AI can read English, Spanish, and Japanese all at once? If we aren't careful, the AI has to work much harder to read non-English languages, chopping them up into tiny, messy pieces!

cards.title: What is Multilingual Tokenization?
cards.body: Multilingual Tokenization is the process of splitting text from many different languages into pieces (tokens) using a single, shared system. To do this fairly, the AI's vocabulary creator must see a balanced mix of all these languages during training.

cards.title: The Cookie Cutter Fit
cards.body: Imagine a cookie cutter shaped like common English syllables. When you press it into English dough, you get perfect, whole cookies. But if you try to press it into a Japanese or Swahili phrase, it doesn't fit! You end up smashing the dough into dozens of tiny crumbs (or character fragments) just to get it through the cutter.

cards.title: Token Count Comparison
cards.body: If a tokenizer is biased toward English, the English word 'banana' might be 1 token. But the Swahili translation 'ndizi' might get broken into 4 separate tokens: 'n', 'di', 'z', 'i'. This makes the AI work four times harder to read the same meaning!

cards.title: The Language Equalizer Myth
cards.body: People think that a tokenizer trained on many languages will naturally treat them all equally. But if the training data has way more English than Hindi, the tokenizer will learn English patterns best, leaving Hindi text heavily fragmented and expensive to process.

cards.title: Balanced vs. Biased Slicing
cards.body: See how a tokenizer's training background changes the way it views different languages.

exercises.prompt: Why is it bad if a tokenizer breaks non-English text into too many small pieces?

options.text: It makes the AI completely forget how to speak English.
It uses up more memory and makes the AI slower to process foreign languages.
It automatically translates all text into emojis.
It decreases the total file size of the AI model.

exercises.explain: When a tokenizer splits words into too many pieces, the AI has to process more tokens overall, which slows down response times and consumes more computational memory.

exercises.prompt: True or False: To make tokenization fair across different languages, we must train the tokenizer on a balanced mix of multilingual text.
exercises.explain: To create a balanced tokenizer, we must train it on a diverse dataset containing representative text from all the languages we want it to support.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the rule of fair tokenization:
exercises.sentence: If a tokenizer is biased towards English, foreign words are split into too many tiny fragments.
exercises.explain: Without balanced training, the tokenizer won't recognize common word patterns in other languages and will default to splitting them into tiny character fragments.

exercises.prompt: Which option shows signs of a tokenizer that is biased against Japanese?

options.text: Option C: Both are equally efficient for the AI to read.

exercises.explain: The AI's tokenization looks normal and efficient in Option A, but Option B shows extreme fragmentation, indicating a lack of native support or training for Japanese.

exercises.prompt: If an AI tokenizer is trained with 95% English text and only 5% Spanish text, what is the main consequence when processing Spanish?

options.text: They require completely different computer chips to process.
English text gets chopped into individual letters while other languages get whole words.
The English text is split into clean, short token sequences, while other languages are chopped into too many tiny fragments.
It becomes completely impossible to translate between the two languages.

exercises.explain: When a tokenizer is trained mainly on one language, other languages suffer from 'over-fragmentation' because they are sliced into tiny, inefficient character-level pieces. Balance is key!
